content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

May 10, 1989

Jane Quan, Administration
County of Alameda Sheriff's Department
1225 Fallon Street, Room 103
Oakland, CA 94612-4381

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-89-182

Dear Ms. Quan:

You have been authorized by Sheriff Plummer to request advice
concerning his reporting requirements under the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").1/

QUESTION

Are travel expenses and per diem received from a nonprofit,
tax-exempt educational and charitable organization
considered a gift for purposes of the Act?

CONCLUSION

Reimbursement for travel expenses and per diem received from
a bona fide educational, academic or charitable organization is
not a gift within the meaning of the Act if the recipient provided
consideration of equal or greater value to the organization.

FACTS

Sheriff Plummer attended a law enforcement mission to Israel
sponsored and financed by the Anti-Defamation League of B'nai
B'rith (the "League"). Sheriff Plummer and nine other top law
enforcement officers from the United States participated with
Israeli Police in a program to share ideas on a variety of law

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Jane Quan, Administration
May 10, 1989
Page 2

enforcement matters, including issues relating to counter-
terrorism. Sheriff Plummer's travel and per diem were paid for by
the League, and the value of those costs was approximately $2,500.

Sheriff Plummer and the others in the group met daily with
officials of various police agencies, both civilian and military,
to discuss terrorism, demonstrations and other actions in which
the police become involved. They discussed police problems unique
to various sights viewed (for example, airports.) Sheriff Plummer
analyzed police functions, and discussed how to handle specific
situations requiring police response, including rock-throwing. He
gave input as a law enforcement expert with 30 years' experience,
including his experience with rock-throwing and demonstrations
involving the People's Park in Berkeley. He also gained
information with respect to the control and monitoring of
terrorist activities.

The Anti-Defamation League is qualified as an educational and
charitable organization by the IRS, qualifying under Section
501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code. The League's goal is to
educate Americans about Israel; promote better interfaith and
intergroup relations; counteract anti-democratic extremism and
strengthen democratic values and structures. The League maintains
speakers' bureaus and has several publications relating to law,
human rights, interreligious concerns and other topics.

ANALYSIS

Section 82030 (a) defines "income," in part, as any "gift,
including any gift of food or beverage...." Section 82030 (b)
lists the following exception to "income":

(2) Salary and reimbursement for
expenses or per diem received from a state,
local, or federal government agency and
reimbursement for travel expenses and per diem
received from a bona fide educational,
academic, or charitable organization.

(Emphasis added.)

Section 82028 defines "gift" as follows:

(a) "Gift" means, except as provided in
subdivision (b), any payment to the extent
that consideration of equal or greater value
is not received....

Subdivision (b) of Section 82028 contains six exceptions to the
definition of "gift." however an exception for gifts of travel and
per diem from bona fide charitable, educational or academic
organizations is not included.

Jane Quan, Administration
May 10, 1989
Page 3

Based on the language of Sections 82028 and 82030, the
exclusion for travel expenses and per diem received from a bona
fide educational, academic, or charitable organization set forth
in Section 82030 is intended to apply to payments made for
consideration and not to gifts. When determining whether or not
an official has received a gift, the only exclusions which apply
are those set forth in the exceptions to gift in Section 82028(b).
(Riddle Advice Letter, No. A-89-200, copy enclosed.)

Under the above interpretation, the burden would shift to
Sheriff Plummer to show that he provided consideration of equal or
greater value. If he does not meet this burden of proof, the
League's payment of his travel expenses and per diem will
constitute a gift. Section 85400 states that an elected of-
ficial may not receive a gift in excess of $1,000.

If Sheriff Plummer has received a gift, he may reimburse the
League for all or a portion of the gift. In that case, the value
of the gift will be reduced by the amount of reimbursement.
(Regulation 18726.1(b), copy enclosed.) The regulation requires
that reimbursement occur within 30 days of the date of receipt of
the gift. However, we do not believe that it would be equitable
to enforce the 30-day rule in this case since your request for
advice was made during the 30-day period. Therefore, if the
travel was a gift, and Sheriff Plummer reimburses the League
within 15 days of the date of this letter, we would consider the
reimbursement to be timely.

Should you have any questions regarding the foregoing, I may
be reached at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel

By: Margaret W. Ellison
Counsel, Legal Division

KED:MWE:aa

Enclosures

County of Alameda
Sheriff's Department

COURTHOUSE, 1225 FALLON STREET, ROOM 103, OAKLAND, CALIFORNIA 94612-4581

415 272-6866

March 17, 1989

Margaret Ellison
California Fair Political Practices Commission
P O Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Dear Ms. Ellison:

Since our telephone conversation of March 16, 1989, I have clarified the ques-
tions I had concerning our department stationery.

We also discussed Sheriff Plummer's attendance at a law enforcement mission to
Israel this month sponsored and financed by the Anti-Defamation League of
B'nai B'rith. Sheriff Plummer and nine other top law enforcement officers
from the United States participated with Israeli Police authorities in a pro-
gram to share ideas on a variety of law enforcement matters, including issues
relating to counter-terrorism.

The Anti-Defamation League is listed as a charity pursuant to IRS regula-
tions. The League's stated goal is to educate Americans about Israel; promote
better interfaith and intergroup relations; counteract anti-democratic
extremism and strengthen democratic values and structures. The League main-
tains speaker's bureaus and has several publications relating to law, human
rights, interreligious concerns and other topics. The League also sponsors a
competition for the best books about children written for children and adults.

Please advise if the seminar qualifies as an educational or academic trip and
therefore exempt from disclosure.

Thank you for your assistance.

Sincerely,

Jane Quan
Administration

/jq

CHARLES C. PLUMMER
SHERIFF

★13862★ ANTI-DEFAMATION LEAGUE OF B'NAI B'RITH (Civil Rights)
(ADL)
823 United Nations Plaza                    Phone: (212) 490-2525
New York, NY 10017                         Nathan Perlmutter, Natl.Dir.
Founded: 1913. Staff: 399. Budget: $23,000,000. "To stop the defama-
tion of Jewish people and to secure justice and fair treatment to all citizens
alike." Educates Americans about Israel; promotes better interfaith and
intergroup relations; works against anti-Semitism, counteracts anti-demo-
cratic extremism and strengthens democratic values and structures. Sponsors
the Jansz Korczak Literary Competition for the best books about children
written for children and adults. Bestows three annual awards: America's
Democratic Legacy Award, the Joseph Prize for human rights, Hubert H.
Humphrey Free Amendment Freedoms Prize. Maintains speakers' bureau and
30 regional offices. Divisions: Civil Rights; Communications; Community Ser-
vice; Development; Intergroup Relations; Leadership; Publications; (1) Anti-
Defamation League Bulletin, (2) Facts, (3) Law and Order Quarterly (Anti-
Interreligious Bulletin, quarterly; (4) Facts, quarterly, (5) Law, quarterly; (6)
Middle East Insight, quarterly, (7) Injustice Encounter, quarterly, (8) Rights,
quarterly; (9) Bulletin, periodic; also publishes monographs, articles, and
teaching materials. Associated: (1965) Institute for Democratic Education.
Convention/Meeting: semiannual.

★13863★ ASIAN AMERICAN LEGAL DEFENSE AND EDUCATION FUND
(Civil Rights) (AALDEF)
99 Hudson St.                                   Phone: (212) 966-5932
New York, NY 10013                            Margaret Fung, Exec.Dir.
Founded: 1974. Attorneys, legal workers, and members of the community
who seek to employ legal and educational methods to attack critical problems
in Asian-American communities. Provides financial legal counseling and rep-
resentation in the areas of immigration, employment, housing, education, and
concern immigration, employment, housing, voting rights, and racially-
motivated violence against Asian-Americans. Litigates cases that have the
potential of improving the quality of life in the community. Sponsors work-
shops, seminars and training sessions to inform community workers and resi-
dents of their rights and benefits before legal problems arise. Conducts law
school intern program to provide students with legal experience in a commu-
nity setting. Publications: Newsletter, semiannual; also publishes pamphlets
(in Chinese, Korean, Japanese, and English). Convention/Meeting: quarterly
conference.

dividuals and local community organizations devoted to monitoring and im-
proving media images of Asian-Americans. Is established to protest the per-
petuation of stereotypes and racism in the media. Sponsors advocacy program and
educational outreach program regarding Asian-Americans. Maintains speakers'
bureau. Committees: Media. Formerly: (1985) Coalition of Asians for the
Charlie Chan. Convention/Meeting: annual conference. Presently inactive.

March 29, 1989


Jane Quan
Alameda County Sheriff's Department
Courthouse
1225 Fallon Street, Room 103
Oakland, CA 94612-4381

Re: Letter No. 89-182

Dear Ms. Quan:

Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform Act
was received on March 27, 1989 by the Fair Political Practices
Commission. If you have any questions about our advice request,
you may contact Margaret Ellison an attorney in the Legal
Division, directly at (916) 322-5901.

We try to answer all advice requests promptly. Therefore,
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, or
more information is needed, you should expect a response within 21
working days if your request seeks formal written advice. If more
information is needed, the person assigned to prepare a response
to your request will contact you shortly to advise you as to
information needed. If your request is for informal assistance,
we will answer it as quickly as we can. (See Commission
Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 18329).)

You also should be aware that your letter and our response
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

Very truly yours,

Diane M. Griffiths
General Counsel

428 I Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Ms. Ellison:

Trip was 3/5 to 3/14. Possible reimbursement
must be made before 4/5?

Please call me when you have had a chance to
review this.

Thank you.

Jane Quan

April 7, 1989


Jane Quan
Alameda County Sheriff's Department
1225 Fallon Street, Room 103
Oakland, CA 94612-4381

Re:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-89-182

Dear Ms. Quan:

     With respect to your request for advice on behalf of
Sheriff Plummer, please be advised that we are extending time
for gift reimbursement pending our determination in this
matter.  This letter will acknowledge that you sought advice
within the 30-day period allowed for return or reimbursement
of gifts.  (Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations, Regulation 18726.4.)  We need to review certain
facts in connection with your case and anticipate that our
written advice will not be completed within the 30-day
reimbursement period.  We appreciate your continued
cooperation in this matter.

     Should you have any questions regarding the foregoing, I
may be reached at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Diane M. Griffiths
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]

                                   By:  Margaret W. Ellison
                                        Counsel, Legal Division

DMG:MWE:aa

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

June 9, 1989


Douglas H. Hickling
Office of County Counsel
1221 Oak Street, Room 463
Oakland, CA 94612

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-89-182

Dear Mr. Hickling:

This will confirm that Sheriff Plummer is not required to
reimburse the Anti-Defamation League for travel and per diem
relating to the trip to Israel that is in excess of $1,000.
Because of clarifications by the Commission at the June 6th meet-
ing, we are now advising that the $1,000 limitation contained in
Government Code Section 85400 applies only to payments made for a
speech, article or published work related to the governmental
process, excluding actual travel expenses and reasonable
subsistence directly in connection therewith.

There is still an issue regarding whether or not the sheriff
will be required to report the reimbursement for travel and per
diem as a gift on his statement of economic interest. That issue
will be presented to the Commission at its meeting on July 12,
1989.

If you have any questions regarding the above, please contact
me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Margaret W. Ellison
Counsel, Legal Division

KED/MWE:aa:HICKLING1

428 I Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

COUNTY COUNSEL
FOURTH FLOOR, ADMINISTRATION BUILDING, 1221 OAK STREET RICHARD J. MOORE
OAKLAND, CALIFORNIA 94612 • TELEPHONE 272-6700 COUNTY COUNSEL

May 24, 1989

Margaret W. Ellison, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804-0807

Re: Your File No. A-89-182

Dear Ms. Ellison:

This is to confirm the telephone conversation which we had earlier today
in which you advised me that Sheriff Charles Plummer would be granted an
extension of 15 days through June 9, 1989, in which to reimburse b'nai B'rith
for the value of his recent trip to Israel. It is our understanding that the
Fair Political Practices Commission at its meeting on June 6, 1989 may take
action with respect to certain regulations that could have an impact upon the
Sheriff's obligation to reimburse that organization.

Very truly yours,

RICHARD J. MOORE,
County Counsel

By [illegible]
DOUGLAS HUCKLING,
Chief Assistant County Counsel

section 82030 (a) defines "income," in part, as any "gift, 
including any gift of food or beverage .... " 
section 82030 (b) 
lists the following exception to "income": 
(2) 
Salary and reimbursement for 
expenses or per diem received from a state, 
local, or federal government agency and 
reimbursement for travel expenses and per diem 
received from a bona fide educational, 
academic, or charitable organization. 
(Emphasis added.) 
section 82028 defines "gift" as follows: 
(a) 
"Gift" means, except as provided in 
subdivision (b), any payment to the extent 
that consideration of equal or greater value 
is not received .... 
Subdivision (b) of section 82028 contains six exceptions to the 
definition of "gift," however an exception for gifts of travel and 
per diem from bona fide charitable, educational or academic 
organizations is not included. 


Jane Quan, Administration 
May 10, 1989  
Based on the language of sections 82028 and 82030, the 
exclusion for travel expenses and per diem received from a bona 
fide educational, academic, or charitable organization set forth 
in Section 82030 is intended to apply to payments made for 
consideration and not to gifts. 
When determining whether or not 
an official has received a gift, the only exclusions which apply 
are those set forth in the exceptions to gift in Section 82028(b). 
(Riddle Advice Letter, No. A-89-200, copy enclosed.) 
Under the above interpretation, the burden would shift to 
Sheriff Plummer to show that he provided consideration of equal or 
greater value. 
If he does not meet this burden of proof, the 
League's payment of his travel expenses and per diem will 
constitute a gift. 
Section 85400 states that an elected of-
ficeholder may not receive a gift in excess of $1,000. 
If Sheriff Plummer has received a gift, he may reimburse the 
League for all or a portion of the gift. 
In that case, the value 
of the gift will be reduced by the amount of reimbursement. 
(Regulation 18726.1(b), copy enclosed.) 
The regulation requires 
that reimbursement occur within 30 days of the date of receipt of 
the gift. 
However, we do not believe that it would be equitable 
to enforce the 30-day rule in this case since your request for 
advice was made during the 30-day period. 
Therefore, if the 
travel was a gift, and Sheriff Plummer reimburses the League 
within 15 days of the date of this letter, we would consider the 
reimbursement to ~e timely.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Are travel expenses and per diem received from a nonprofit, 
tax-exempt educational and charitable organization 
considered a gift for purposes of the Act?

CONCLUSION: Reimbursement for travel expenses and per diem received from 
a bona fide educational, academic or charitable organization is 
not a gift within the meaning of the Act if the recipient provided 
consideration of equal or greater value to the organization.

The Anti-Defamation League is qualified as an educational and charitable organization by the IRS, qualifying under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code. The League's goal is to educate Americans about Israel; promote better interfaith and intergroup relations; counteract anti-democratic extremism and strengthen democratic values and structures. The League maintains speakers' bureaus and has several publications relating to law, human rights, interreligious concerns and other topics. ANALYSIS Section 82030 (a) defines "income," in part, as any "gift, including any gift of food or beverage...." Section 82030 (b) lists the following exception to "income": (2) Salary and reimbursement for expenses or per diem received from a state, local, or federal government agency and reimbursement for travel expenses and per diem received from a bona fide educational, academic, or charitable organization.
